ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tempura class (FOODON_03302050); no specific kakiage subclass exists. Mapped to the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tempura — battered and deep-fried mixed seafood and vegetable dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers tempura as a battered fried food category; no kakiage-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Mapped to closest defensible category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for kakiage or tempura fritter identified in the published SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

main_ingredients: tempura batter (wheat flour, egg, cold water)
kabayaki sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)
allergens: may contain tree nuts (truffle oil formulations)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of kakiage with truffle kabayaki sauce weighs approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tempura, shrimp and vegetable, restaurant-prepared (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for kabayaki sauce components (soy sauce, mirin, sugar)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for kakiage with truffle kabayaki sauce exists. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for shrimp and vegetable tempura (deep-fried, battered) and kabayaki sauce components. The input record values (energy 220 kcal, protein 8 g, fat 14 g, carbohydrate 16 g, sodium 480 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA tempura analogues and are retained with medium confidence. Truffle component contributes negligible macronutrients at typical usage levels. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and remaining micronutrients not reported in the closest USDA analogue for this preparation; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Tempura batter (wheat flour-based deep-fried batter) analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat flour-based fried batter foods.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kakiage with truffle kabayaki sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the analogous food category of wheat flour-based deep-fried batter (tempura batter), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The kabayaki sauce contributes sugar (4 g/100 g) which modestly elevates the effective GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (16 g/100 g × 250 g = 40 g) / 100 = 26.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg per 100 g; 1,200 mg per standard 250 g serving — well above the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium from soy-based kabayaki sauce and seasoned batter; if consumed, request sauce on the side and restrict portion to no more than 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (estimated 65) with high glycemic load per serving (GL 26); refined wheat-flour tempura batter and sugar-containing kabayaki sauce elevate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this dish due to its high glycemic load (GL 26 per serving) from refined tempura batter and mirin-sugar kabayaki sauce; if consumed, pair with a high-fibre side dish and request reduced sauce to moderate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (480 mg/100 g), saturated fat (2.4 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), and cholesterol (40 mg/100 g) from deep-frying in vegetable oil and soy-based sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the combination of high sodium, saturated fat, trans fat from deep-frying, and cholesterol, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; a grilled or steamed seafood and vegetable alternative is strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium 480 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and the high sodium content worsens tissue swelling; request steamed or grilled seafood and vegetables as an alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kakiage with Truffle Kabayaki Sauce is a Japanese deep-fried tempura dish in which mixed vegetables (such as burdock, carrot, and onion) and seafood (typically shrimp and squid) are combined into a loose fritter using a light wheat-flour and egg batter, then deep-fried until golden and crisp. The dish is finished with a rich kabayaki sauce — a Japanese glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar — elevated here with truffle extract or truffle oil for an umami-forward, aromatic finish. The texture contrasts a shatteringly crisp exterior with a tender, moist interior of vegetables and seafood. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 220 kcal, 8 g protein, 14 g fat (2.4 g saturated, 0.2 g trans), 16 g carbohydrate, 4 g sugar, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 480 mg sodium. The high sodium content (480 mg/100 g) and medium glycemic index (estimated 65, GL 26 per 250 g serving) make this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The deep-frying process also generates advanced glycation end-products relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. This dish is served in Japanese restaurants in Singapore and is culturally associated with Japanese tempura cuisine. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with wheat, egg, crustacean, mollusc, or soy allergies.

Japanese tempura fritter with truffle sauce
What is kabayaki sauce made of?
meal_context: À la carte Japanese tempura dish
Part of a Japanese set meal (teishoku)

confidence_reason: No exact entry for kakiage with truffle kabayaki sauce exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central analogues for shrimp and vegetable tempura and kabayaki sauce components. The input record values are consistent with these analogues and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for wheat flour-based deep-fried batter foods. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature of the dish and the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite deep-fried dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the closest USDA FoodData Central analogue for this composite tempura preparation. 3. GI estimated at 65 (medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous category for wheat flour-based deep-fried batter; no direct GI measurement for kakiage with truffle kabayaki sauce is available. 4. The dish name '701D' in the input is a restaurant menu code and is not part of the canonical food name; it is retained in aliases only. 5. Halal status is unconfirmed as kabayaki sauce contains mirin (rice wine) and sake; is_halal is set to false pending confirmation. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.2 g/100 g, consistent with small amounts of industrially produced trans fats from deep-frying in partially hydrogenated oils; this is a low but non-zero value supported by USDA tempura analogues. 7. Although the primary cuisine is Japanese, this dish is sold in Singapore and the validator requires Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases for Singapore-served dishes. Chinese simplified and traditional names, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi translations have been added to local_names and aliases accordingly. All aliases_by_language string values verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Added Chinese simplified ('炸什锦天妇罗配松露蒲烧酱'), Chinese traditional ('炸什錦天婦羅配松露蒲燒醬'), Malay ('Kakiage dengan Sos Kabayaki Truffle'), Tamil ('காகியாகே டிரஃபிள் கபயாகி சாஸுடன்' and 'காகியாகே'), and Hindi ('काकियागे ट्रफल कबायाकी सॉस के साथ' and 'काकியागे') to local_names and to both aliases_by_language and flat aliases array to satisfy G6 and Q8 validator requirements for Singapore-served dish context.
